ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pork Bulgogi; closest parent 'pork food product' (FOODON_03302757) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Pork Bulgogi (Korean marinated grilled pork). 'Pig meat, cooked' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically covers Pork Bulgogi as a prepared dish. No code assigned.

serving.description: 1 serving (as part of a Korean meal set with rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pork Bulgogi is approximately 150 g of marinated grilled pork, usually served alongside steamed rice and Korean side dishes (banchan).

nutrient_source.title: Pork, bulgogi, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Korean composite reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy); cross-referenced with Korean National Standard Food Composition Table (Rural Development Administration, Korea)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Pork Bulgogi as a composite dish. Values are derived from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked marinated pork (NDB No. 10219, pork loin cooked) and adjusted for the marinade composition (soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, gochujang) using standard Korean recipe proportions consistent with the Korean Rural Development Administration National Standard Food Composition Table (9th revision, 2019). Energy and macros reflect the cooked, marinated, grilled product at approximately 150 g serving size, normalised to per-100 g basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Korean dishes including bulgogi-style marinated pork; analogous estimate based on GI of marinated pork dishes and soy-sauce-based meat preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pork Bulgogi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) marinated pork dishes with soy sauce and sugar marinades typically fall in the low-to-medium GI range (GI 40–55) due to protein and fat attenuating glucose response; (2) the sugar content of the marinade contributes moderate glycemic load; (3) reference: Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, which lists similar marinated meat preparations. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (44 × 12.75) / 100 ≈ 5.6, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 8.5 × 1.5 = 12.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and gochujang marinade (approximately 620 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Pork Bulgogi portion sizes and request reduced-sodium marinade preparations, as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, representing over 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content from sugar-based marinade (8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with low estimated GI of 44
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork Bulgogi has a low estimated glycemic index (GI 44) and moderate glycemic load per serving (GL ~5.6), making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional rice or with portion-controlled rice; however, the sugar content of the marinade should be factored into total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (3.8 g per 100 g) and high sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate Pork Bulgogi intake due to its saturated fat content (3.8 g/100 g) and high sodium load; choosing leaner pork cuts and reduced-sodium soy sauce in the marinade can improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Bulgogi (돼지불고기, Dwaeji Bulgogi) is a classic Korean dish consisting of thinly sliced pork marinated in a flavourful mixture of soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, sugar, and gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste), then grilled or stir-fried over high heat. The name 'bulgogi' literally means 'fire meat' in Korean, and the pork version is distinguished from the more internationally known beef bulgogi by its spicier, bolder marinade profile. The dish delivers a characteristic combination of sweet, savoury, and mildly spicy flavours with a slightly caramelised exterior from the sugar in the marinade. Texturally, the pork is tender and juicy when properly marinated, with a lightly charred surface. Pork Bulgogi is a staple of Korean home cooking and restaurant menus, typically served as part of a full Korean meal set (한식) alongside steamed white rice and an assortment of banchan (side dishes) such as kimchi, spinach namul, and bean sprouts. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 218 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 12 g fat (of which 3.8 g is saturated), and 8.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high at approximately 620 mg per 100 g due to the soy sauce and gochujang marinade, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (GI ~44), making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with controlled carbohydrate accompaniments. In Singapore, Pork Bulgogi is commonly available at Korean restaurants and food delivery platforms, often served as part of a set meal with rice and side dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork bulgogi?
Is pork bulgogi good for diabetics?
Is pork bulgogi high in sodium?
meal_context: Korean set meal with rice and banchan

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Pork Bulgogi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork data cross-referenced with Korean RDA food composition data for the marinade contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to similar marinated pork preparations. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the reference sources used for this composite dish adaptation. 2. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy cooked pork entry used as the primary anchor. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this preparation. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this preparation. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this composite dish. 6. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pork Bulgogi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce and gochujang in the marinade; actual sodium may vary by restaurant recipe. 8. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 9. Nutrient values represent the cooked marinated pork component only, excluding rice and banchan side dishes served alongside.
